Experimental results show that DFR allows LLMs to decode latent spatial patterns and perform accurate zero-shot reasoning across tasks, while significantly improving efficiency compared to text-based baselines. Our results demonstrate that treating embeddings as primary data inputs, provides a more direct, efficient, and scalable approach to multimodal geospatial intelligence.

</details>


### [88] [Decompose, Look, and Reason: Reinforced Latent Reasoning for VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07518)
*Mengdan Zhu,Senhao Cheng,Liang Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces "Decompose, Look, and Reason" (DLR), a model improving vision-language reasoning through reinforced latent reasoning and a novel Spherical Gaussian Latent Policy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Vision-Language Models struggle with complex visual reasoning due to visual information loss in textual chains of thought and insufficient tools for semantics in multi-step reasoning.

Method: The proposed DLR framework dynamically decomposes queries into textual premises, extracts premise-conditioned continuous visual latents, and deduces answers through grounded rationales. A three-stage training pipeline and a novel Spherical Gaussian Latent Policy are used.

Result: DLR consistently outperforms strong baselines on vision-centric benchmarks, showing superior accuracy and stepwise interpretability.

Conclusion: DLR enhances complex visual reasoning in Vision-Language Models, offering both performance improvement and better interpretability.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models often struggle with complex visual reasoning due to the visual information loss in textual CoT. Existing methods either add the cost of tool calls or rely on localized patch-based embeddings that are insufficient to extract semantics in multi-step reasoning. We propose \emph{"Decompose, Look, and Reason" (DLR)}, a reinforced latent reasoning framework that dynamically decomposes queries into textual premises, extracts premise-conditioned continuous visual latents, and deduces answers through grounded rationales. We introduce a three-stage training pipeline and propose a novel Spherical Gaussian Latent Policy to enable effective exploration in the latent space. Extensive experiments on vision-centric benchmarks show that DLR consistently outperforms strong baselines, including text-only, interleaved multimodal CoT, and latent reasoning methods, while providing superior stepwise interpretability.

</details>


### [89] [EMSDialog: Synthetic Multi-person Emergency Medical Service Dialogue Generation from Electronic Patient Care Reports via Multi-LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07549)
*Xueren Ge,Sahil Murtaza,Anthony Cortez,Homa Alemzadeh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces EMSDialog, a synthetic dataset of multi-speaker EMS conversations, to aid conversational diagnosis prediction and demonstrates its effectiveness in improving model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The aim is to develop models capable of diagnosis prediction in dynamic clinical conversations while addressing the lack of suitable datasets that capture multi-party interactions and annotations.

Method: The authors designed a multi-agent generation pipeline grounded in ePCR data to iteratively plan, generate, and refine emergency medical service (EMS) dialogues, ensuring factual accuracy and topic coherence.

Result: The resulting EMSDialog dataset contains 4,414 synthetic EMS conversations with multi-party annotations. Evaluations confirmed its realism and utility, and models trained with EMSDialog showed improvements in diagnosis prediction accuracy and stability.

Conclusion: EMSDialog provides a valuable resource for advancing conversational diagnosis prediction, overcoming existing dataset limitations and enhancing model capabilities.

Abstract: Conversational diagnosis prediction requires models to track evolving evidence in streaming clinical conversations and decide when to commit to a diagnosis. Existing medical dialogue corpora are largely dyadic or lack the multi-party workflow and annotations needed for this setting. We introduce an ePCR-grounded, topic-flow-based multi-agent generation pipeline that iteratively plans, generates, and self-refines dialogues with rule-based factual and topic flow checks. The pipeline yields EMSDialog, a dataset of 4,414 synthetic multi-speaker EMS conversations based on a real-world ePCR dataset, annotated with 43 diagnoses, speaker roles, and turn-level topics. Human and LLM evaluations confirm high quality and realism of EMSDialog using both utterance- and conversation-level metrics. Results show that EMSDialog-augmented training improves accuracy, timeliness, and stability of EMS conversational diagnosis prediction.

</details>


### [90] [TR-EduVSum: A Turkish-Focused Dataset and Consensus Framework for Educational Video Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07553)
*Figen Eğin,Aytuğ Onan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces AutoMUP, a method for creating automatic gold-standard summaries of Turkish educational videos using multiple human summaries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To automate and standardize the generation of gold-standard summaries for educational content.

Method: AutoMUP uses clustering, embedding, and statistical modeling to extract consensus-based meaning units from human summaries. It creates a dataset called TR-EduVSum and generates summaries weighted by consensus.

Result: Summaries generated by AutoMUP significantly align with summaries from advanced language models like GPT-5.1 and Flash 2.5.

Conclusion: AutoMUP is effective for creating reproducible gold-standard summaries and can be adapted for other Turkic languages at a low cost.

Abstract: This study presents a framework for generating the gold-standard summary fully automatically and reproducibly based on multiple human summaries of Turkish educational videos. Within the scope of the study, a new dataset called TR-EduVSum was created, encompassing 82 Turkish course videos in the field of "Data Structures and Algorithms" and containing a total of 3281 independent human summaries. Inspired by existing pyramid-based evaluation approaches, the AutoMUP (Automatic Meaning Unit Pyramid) method is proposed, which extracts consensus-based content from multiple human summaries. AutoMUP clusters the meaning units extracted from human summaries using embedding, statistically models inter-participant agreement, and generates graded summaries based on consensus weight. In this framework, the gold summary corresponds to the highest-consensus AutoMUP configuration, constructed from the most frequently supported meaning units across human summaries. Experimental results show that AutoMUP summaries exhibit high semantic overlap with robust LLM (Large Language Model) summaries such as Flash 2.5 and GPT-5.1. Furthermore, ablation studies clearly demonstrate the decisive role of consensus weight and clustering in determining summary quality. The proposed approach can be generalized to other Turkic languages at low cost.

</details>


### [91] [Reasoning-Based Refinement of Unsupervised Text Clusters with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07562)
*Tunazzina Islam*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a new framework that uses large language models (LLMs) for refining unsupervised clustering outputs, focusing on improving coherence, reducing redundancy, and labeling clusters interpretably.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of unsupervised clustering methods, such as incoherent, redundant, or poorly grounded clusters, and to achieve validation without reliance on labeled data.

Method: The proposed framework uses LLMs in three stages: coherence verification of clusters, redundancy adjudication by merging/rejecting clusters, and unsupervised label grounding for cluster interpretation.

Result: The framework improves cluster coherence, reduces redundancy, and enhances labeling quality on real-world datasets compared to classical and recent baselines, with strong agreement between human evaluation and LLM labels.

Conclusion: The results demonstrate the potential of LLM-based reasoning frameworks to validate and refine clustering outputs, offering a robust and interpretable solution for analyzing large text datasets without supervision.

Abstract: Unsupervised methods are widely used to induce latent semantic structure from large text collections, yet their outputs often contain incoherent, redundant, or poorly grounded clusters that are difficult to validate without labeled data. We propose a reasoning-based refinement framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) not as embedding generators, but as semantic judges that validate and restructure the outputs of arbitrary unsupervised clustering algorithms.Our framework introduces three reasoning stages: (i) coherence verification, where LLMs assess whether cluster summaries are supported by their member texts; (ii) redundancy adjudication, where candidate clusters are merged or rejected based on semantic overlap; and (iii) label grounding, where clusters are assigned interpretable labels in a fully unsupervised manner. This design decouples representation learning from structural validation and mitigates common failure modes of embedding-only approaches. We evaluate the framework on real-world social media corpora from two platforms with distinct interaction models, demonstrating consistent improvements in cluster coherence and human-aligned labeling quality over classical topic models and recent representation-based baselines. Human evaluation shows strong agreement with LLM-generated labels, despite the absence of gold-standard annotations. We further conduct robustness analyses under matched temporal and volume conditions to assess cross-platform stability. Beyond empirical gains, our results suggest that LLM-based reasoning can serve as a general mechanism for validating and refining unsupervised semantic structure, enabling more reliable and interpretable analyses of large text collections without supervision.

</details>


### [92] [CAMO: A Class-Aware Minority-Optimized Ensemble for Robust Language Model Evaluation on Imbalanced Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07583)
*Mohamed Ehab,Ali Hamdi,Khaled Shaban*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CAMO, a novel ensemble method addressing class imbalance issues in categorization tasks, particularly improving minority class predictions and F1-score.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Class imbalance hampers performance in real-world categorization tasks, necessitating methods that enhance minority class predictions while maintaining overall results.

Method: The CAMO ensemble dynamically boosts minority class predictions via vote distributions, confidence calibration, and inter-model uncertainty. Evaluation was conducted on domain-specific benchmarks with comparisons against established algorithms.

Result: With fine-tuned models, CAMO outperforms existing methods in macro F1-score on imbalanced datasets, establishing a new benchmark.

Conclusion: CAMO is a reliable and domain-neutral solution for imbalanced categorization, highlighting the interplay between ensemble and model properties.

Abstract: Real-world categorization is severely hampered by class imbalance because traditional ensembles favor majority classes, which lowers minority performance and overall F1-score. We provide a unique ensemble technique for imbalanced problems called CAMO (Class-Aware Minority-Optimized).Through a hierarchical procedure that incorporates vote distributions, confidence calibration, and inter model uncertainty, CAMO dynamically boosts underrepresented classes while preserving and amplifying minority forecasts. We verify CAMO on two highly unbalanced, domain-specific benchmarks: the DIAR-AI/Emotion dataset and the ternary BEA 2025 dataset. We benchmark against seven proven ensemble algorithms using eight different language models (three LLMs and five SLMs) under zero-shot and fine-tuned settings .With refined models, CAMO consistently earns the greatest strict macro F1-score, setting a new benchmark. Its benefit works in concert with model adaptation, showing that the best ensemble choice depends on model properties .This proves that CAMO is a reliable, domain-neutral framework for unbalanced categorization.

</details>


### [93] [ADAG: Automatically Describing Attribution Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07615)
*Aryaman Arora,Zhengxuan Wu,Jacob Steinhardt,Sarah Schwettmann*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces ADAG, a new automated method for analyzing circuits in language models, explaining their behavior without relying on manual interpretation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the reliance on ad-hoc human interpretation in circuit tracing research, which limits scalability and consistency in analyzing the causal contributions of features in language models.

Method: The authors propose a fully automated pipeline, ADAG, which uses attribution profiles to quantify the role of features, employs a clustering algorithm to group features, and leverages an LLM explainer-simulator for generating explanations of feature roles.

Result: The method successfully replicates human-analyzed circuit tracing tasks and identifies steerable clusters responsible for a harmful jailbreak in the Llama 3.1 8B Instruct model.

Conclusion: ADAG offers a scalable and interpretable approach to understanding the computations within language models, enabling automated discovery of both functional and harmful feature clusters.

Abstract: In language model interpretability research, \textbf{circuit tracing} aims to identify which internal features causally contributed to a particular output and how they affected each other, with the goal of explaining the computations underlying some behaviour. However, all prior circuit tracing work has relied on ad-hoc human interpretation of the role that each feature in the circuit plays, via manual inspection of data artifacts such as the dataset examples the component activates on. We introduce \textbf{ADAG}, an end-to-end pipeline for describing these attribution graphs which is fully automated. To achieve this, we introduce \textit{attribution profiles} which quantify the functional role of a feature via its input and output gradient effects. We then introduce a novel clustering algorithm for grouping features, and an LLM explainer--simulator setup which generates and scores natural-language explanations of the functional role of these feature groups. We run our system on known human-analysed circuit-tracing tasks and recover interpretable circuits, and further show ADAG can find steerable clusters which are responsible for a harmful advice jailbreak in Llama 3.1 8B Instruct.

</details>


### [94] [DIVERSED: Relaxed Speculative Decoding via Dynamic Ensemble Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07622)
*Ziyi Wang,Siva Rajesh Kasa,Ankith M S,Santhosh Kumar Kasa,Jiaru Zou,Sumit Negi,Ruqi Zhang,Nan Jiang,Qifan Song*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces DIVERSED, a framework to enhance the efficiency of speculative decoding in language models by relaxing verification criteria while maintaining output quality, achieving notable improvements in speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speculative decoding processes in language models are bottlenecked by strict verification steps that reduce token acceptance rates, slowing down overall inference speed despite the parallel drafting of tokens.

Method: The proposed framework, DIVERSED, utilizes an ensemble-based verifier that integrates draft and target model distributions, assigning weights tailored to the task and context, and relaxes the strict verification constraints.

Result: DIVERSED outperforms conventional speculative decoding techniques in efficiency, maintaining high-quality outputs while significantly reducing inference time.

Conclusion: By relaxing rigid verification criteria and leveraging a dynamic ensemble-based verifier, DIVERSED offers a more time-efficient speculative decoding approach without sacrificing the generation quality of language models.

Abstract: Speculative decoding is an effective technique for accelerating large language model inference by drafting multiple tokens in parallel. In practice, its speedup is often bottlenecked by a rigid verification step that strictly enforces the accepted token distribution to exactly match the target model. This constraint leads to the rejection of many plausible tokens, lowering the acceptance rate and limiting overall time speedup. To overcome this limitation, we propose Dynamic Verification Relaxed Speculative Decoding (DIVERSED), a relaxed verification framework that improves time efficiency while preserving generation quality. DIVERSED learns an ensemble-based verifier that blends the draft and target model distributions with a task-dependent and context-dependent weight. We provide theoretical justification for our approach and demonstrate empirically that DIVERSED achieves substantially higher inference efficiency compared to standard speculative decoding methods. Code is available at: https://github.com/comeusr/diversed.

</details>


### [95] [Efficient and Effective Internal Memory Retrieval for LLM-Based Healthcare Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07659)
*Mingchen Li,Jiatan Huang,Zonghai Yao,Hong yu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Keys to Knowledge (K2K), a method that internally encodes clinical knowledge in language models for efficient and accurate healthcare applications, overcoming latency and reliability issues of external retrieval systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLMs face challenges like hallucinations and lack of detailed medical context in clinical settings, and RAG-based solutions are too slow for real-time medical use.

Method: K2K encodes clinical knowledge directly into a model's parameters, enabling quick internal access while enhancing retrieval with activation-guided probes and cross-attention reranking.

Result: K2K achieved state-of-the-art performance on four healthcare outcome prediction benchmarks, demonstrating high reliability and efficiency.

Conclusion: By addressing both speed and accuracy, K2K offers a promising solution for deploying LLMs in time-sensitive clinical environments.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) hold significant promise for healthcare, yet their reliability in high-stakes clinical settings is often compromised by hallucinations and a lack of granular medical context. While Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) can mitigate these issues, standard supervised pipelines require computationally intensive searches over massive external knowledge bases, leading to high latency that is impractical for time-sensitive care. To address this, we introduce Keys to Knowledge (K2K), a novel framework that replaces external retrieval with internal, key-based knowledge access. By encoding essential clinical information directly into the model's parameter space, K2K enables rapid retrieval from internal key-value memory without inference-time overhead. We further enhance retrieval quality through activation-guided probe construction and cross-attention reranking. Experimental results demonstrate that K2K achieves state-of-the-art performance across four benchmark healthcare outcome prediction datasets.

</details>


### [96] [Detecting HIV-Related Stigma in Clinical Narratives Using Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07717)
*Ziyi Chen,Yasir Khan,Mengyuan Zhang,Cheng Peng,Mengxian Lyu,Yiyang Liu,Krishna Vaddiparti,Robert L Cook,Mattia Prosperi,Yonghui Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper develops an NLP tool for identifying HIV stigma in clinical notes using large language models, and demonstrates promising results, particularly with few-shot prompting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of tools for extracting and categorizing HIV stigma-related experiences from clinical narratives, which are critical for the well-being of people living with HIV.

Method: Clinical notes were annotated for stigma subscales, and various LLMs were evaluated on their ability to predict these annotations under zero-shot and few-shot settings.

Result: GatorTron-large performed best overall with a Micro F1 score of 0.62, while few-shot prompting improved generative LLM performance, achieving up to 0.59 Micro F1.

Conclusion: The study successfully develops the first NLP tool leveraging LLMs for identifying HIV stigma, demonstrating its utility and highlighting performance variations across stigma subscales.

Abstract: Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)-related stigma is a critical psychosocial determinant of health for people living with HIV (PLWH), influencing mental health, engagement in care, and treatment outcomes. Although stigma-related experiences are documented in clinical narratives, there is a lack of off-the-shelf tools to extract and categorize them. This study aims to develop a large language model (LLM)-based tool for identifying HIV stigma from clinical notes. We identified clinical notes from PLWH receiving care at the University of Florida (UF) Health between 2012 and 2022. Candidate sentences were identified using expert-curated stigma-related keywords and iteratively expanded via clinical word embeddings. A total of 1,332 sentences were manually annotated across four stigma subscales: Concern with Public Attitudes, Disclosure Concerns, Negative Self-Image, and Personalized Stigma. We compared GatorTron-large and BERT as encoder-based baselines, and GPT-OSS-20B, LLaMA-8B, and MedGemma-27B as generative LLMs, under zero-shot and few-shot prompting. GatorTron-large achieved the best overall performance (Micro F1 = 0.62). Few-shot prompting substantially improved generative model performance, with 5-shot GPT-OSS-20B and LLaMA-8B achieving Micro-F1 scores of 0.57 and 0.59, respectively. Performance varied by stigma subscale, with Negative Self-Image showing the highest predictability and Personalized Stigma remaining the most challenging. Zero-shot generative inference exhibited non-trivial failure rates (up to 32%). This study develops the first practical NLP tool for identifying HIV stigma in clinical notes.

</details>


### [97] [SepSeq: A Training-Free Framework for Long Numerical Sequence Processing in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07737)
*Jie Sun,Yu Liu,Lu Han,Qiwen Deng,Xiang Shu,Yang Xiao,Xingyu Lu,Jun Zhou,Pengfei Liu,Lintao Ma,Jiancan Wu,Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a framework called Separate Sequence (SepSeq) to address performance issues of Large Language Models (LLMs) with long numerical sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the problem of attention dispersion in LLMs, which causes performance degradation when handling long numerical sequences.

Method: The authors propose SepSeq, a training-free framework that uses strategically inserted separator tokens to act as attention sinks, recalibrating attention to focus on local segments while maintaining global context.

Result: Extensive evaluations across 9 widely-used LLMs show that SepSeq improves accuracy by 35.6% on average and reduces token consumption by 16.4%.

Conclusion: The SepSeq framework successfully mitigates attention dispersion in LLMs, resulting in significant performance improvements and more efficient token usage.

Abstract: While transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) theoretically support massive context windows, they suffer from severe performance degradation when processing long numerical sequences. We attribute this failure to the attention dispersion in the Softmax mechanism, which prevents the model from concentrating attention. To overcome this, we propose Separate Sequence (SepSeq), a training-free, plug-and-play framework to mitigate dispersion by strategically inserting separator tokens. Mechanistically, we demonstrate that separator tokens act as an attention sink, recalibrating attention to focus on local segments while preserving global context. Extensive evaluations on 9 widely-adopted LLMs confirm the effectiveness of our approach: SepSeq yields an average relative accuracy improvement of 35.6% across diverse domains while reducing total inference token consumption by 16.4% on average.

</details>


### [98] [Beyond Social Pressure: Benchmarking Epistemic Attack in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07749)
*Steven Au,Sujit Noronha*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces PPT-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark to assess how large language models respond under different types of philosophical pressure, exposing weaknesses not detected by standard evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing studies on sycophancy in LLMs have mainly focused on disagreement and preference alignment. However, deeper epistemic failures—where models accommodate rather than reason under pressure—remain underexplored.

Method: The authors developed PPT-Bench, using a Philosophical Pressure Taxonomy (PPT) with four pressure types: Epistemic Destabilization, Value Nullification, Authority Inversion, and Identity Dissolution. These are tested across three levels of prompts to measure consistency and conversational behavior.

Result: The study found that different types of philosophical pressure produce distinct inconsistency patterns among five LLMs. Mitigation varied by type and model, with specific methods like persona prompts working well for API models and contrastive decoding for open ones.

Conclusion: Epistemic attacks reveal model weaknesses that are not exposed by standard benchmarks, and effective mitigation strategies depend on both the model and the type of pressure applied.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can shift their answers under pressure in ways that reflect accommodation rather than reasoning. Prior work on sycophancy has focused mainly on disagreement, flattery, and preference alignment, leaving a broader set of epistemic failures less explored. We introduce \textbf{PPT-Bench}, a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating \textit{epistemic attack}, where prompts challenge the legitimacy of knowledge, values, or identity rather than simply opposing a previous answer. PPT-Bench is organized around the Philosophical Pressure Taxonomy (PPT), which defines four types of philosophical pressure: Epistemic Destabilization, Value Nullification, Authority Inversion, and Identity Dissolution. Each item is tested at three layers: a baseline prompt (L0), a single-turn pressure condition (L1), and a multi-turn Socratic escalation (L2). This allows us to measure epistemic inconsistency between L0 and L1, and conversational capitulation in L2. Across five models, these pressure types produce statistically separable inconsistency patterns, suggesting that epistemic attack exposes weaknesses not captured by standard social-pressure benchmarks. Mitigation results are strongly type- and model-dependent: prompt-level anchoring and persona-stability prompts perform best in API settings, while Leading Query Contrastive Decoding is the most reliable intervention for open models.

</details>


### [99] [An Empirical Analysis of Static Analysis Methods for Detection and Mitigation of Code Library Hallucinations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07755)
*Clarissa Miranda-Pena,Andrew Reeson,Cécile Paris,Josiah Poon,Jonathan K. Kummerfeld*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Static analysis tools detect and partially mitigate coding hallucinations by LLMs but fall short of fully solving the issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address hallucinations in LLM-generated code, particularly regarding library usage, where hallucinated non-existent features occur in 8.1-40% of cases.

Method: The study evaluates static analysis tools for their ability to detect and mitigate hallucinations in LLM-generated code across multiple datasets, with manual reviews to determine limitations.

Result: Static analysis tools identify 16-70% of errors and 14-85% of library hallucinations but can only theoretically address 48.5-77% due to inherent limitations.

Conclusion: Static analysis is a low-cost, partial solution for code hallucinations. However, it cannot completely resolve the problem due to inherent limitations in the method.

Abstract: Despite extensive research, Large Language Models continue to hallucinate when generating code, particularly when using libraries. On NL-to-code benchmarks that require library use, we find that LLMs generate code that uses non-existent library features in 8.1-40% of responses.One intuitive approach for detection and mitigation of hallucinations is static analysis. In this paper, we analyse the potential of static analysis tools, both in terms of what they can solve and what they cannot. We find that static analysis tools can detect 16-70% of all errors, and 14-85% of library hallucinations, with performance varying by LLM and dataset. Through manual analysis, we identify cases a static method could not plausibly catch, which gives an upper bound on their potential from 48.5% to 77%. Overall, we show that static analysis methods are cheap method for addressing some forms of hallucination, and we quantify how far short of solving the problem they will always be.

</details>


### [100] [Sensitivity-Positional Co-Localization in GQA Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07766)
*Manoj Chandrashekar Rao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores the structural aspects of Grouped Query Attention (GQA) transformers and finds that high task sensitivity and positional encoding adaptation do not co-occur in the same layers. This contradicts a proposed hypothesis but still yields strong results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether layers highly sensitive to task performance coincide with layers where positional encoding adaptation has the most impact, termed the co-localization hypothesis, in GQA transformers.

Method: The authors test their hypothesis using a novel correctness-differential hidden-state metric, along with two techniques: LSLoRA (layer-restricted LoRA) and GARFA (RoPE Frequency Adaptation), on a Llama 3.1 8B model, involving ablation studies across six benchmarks.

Result: Strong anti-localization between task-sensitive and positional-influence layers was observed (Spearman r_s = -0.735). However, focusing interventions on sensitivity-identified layers still enhanced performance significantly across benchmarks, nearing state-of-the-art results.

Conclusion: Despite disproving the co-localization hypothesis, strategic adaptation in task-sensitive layers achieves compelling performance gains, suggesting disentangled functions for sensitivity and positional adaptation in GQA transformers.

Abstract: We investigate a fundamental structural question in Grouped Query Attention (GQA) transformers: do the layers most sensitive to task correctness coincide with the layers where positional encoding adaptation has the greatest leverage? We term this the co-localization hypothesis and test it on Llama 3.1 8B, a 32-layer GQA model with a 4:1 query-to-key-value head ratio. We introduce \LSLORA, which restricts LoRA adaptation to layers identified via a novel correctness-differential hidden-state metric, and GARFA (GQA-Aware RoPE Frequency Adaptation), which attaches 8 learnable per-KV-head scalar multipliers to each targeted layer. Contrary to the co-localization hypothesis, we discover strong anti-localization: task-sensitive layers concentrate in the late network ($\ell\in\{23\text{-}31\}$) while RoPE-influential layers dominate the early network ($\ell\in\{0\text{-}9\}$), yielding Spearman $r_s = -0.735$ ($p = 1.66\times10^{-6}$). Despite this anti-localization, a 4-way cross-layer ablation shows that applying both interventions to the sensitivity-identified layers outperforms all alternative configurations by 4-16 percentage points across six diverse benchmarks (MMLU, GPQA, HumanEval+, MATH, MGSM, ARC), approaching Claude 3.5 Haiku on HumanEval+ (67.1% vs. 68.3%) at \$100 total compute cost.

</details>


### [101] [Synthetic Data for any Differentiable Target](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08423)
*Tristan Thrush,Sung Min Park,Herman Brunborg,Luke Bailey,Marcel Roed,Neil Band,Christopher Potts,Tatsunori Hashimoto*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces Dataset Policy Gradient (DPG), a reinforcement learning method for precisely controlling language models using synthetic data to achieve specific results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to explore the limitations of controlling language models via synthetic training data and to identify methods for optimizing synthetic data generators to shape model properties effectively.

Method: The authors develop DPG, a reinforcement learning approach that uses higher-order gradients to optimize synthetic data generators for producing datasets targeted at improving specific metrics on a target model during supervised fine-tuning.

Result: Demonstrations show that DPG enables synthetic training data to directly influence specific properties of the target language model, such as embedding QR codes, patterns, or UUIDs, rephrasing inputs, and reducing weight norms.

Conclusion: DPG is a powerful and versatile tool for affecting language model properties through synthetic data, demonstrating high precision and flexibility in optimization.

Abstract: What are the limits of controlling language models via synthetic training data? We develop a reinforcement learning (RL) primitive, the Dataset Policy Gradient (DPG), which can precisely optimize synthetic data generators to produce a dataset of targeted examples. When used for supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of a target model, these examples cause the target model to do well on a differentiable metric of our choice. Our approach achieves this by taking exact data attribution via higher-order gradients and using those scores as policy gradient rewards. We prove that this procedure closely approximates the true, intractable gradient for the synthetic data generator. To illustrate the potential of DPG, we show that, using only SFT on generated examples, we can cause the target model's LM head weights to (1) embed a QR code, (2) embed the pattern $\texttt{67}$, and (3) have lower $\ell^2$ norm. We additionally show that we can cause the generator to (4) rephrase inputs in a new language and (5) produce a specific UUID, even though neither of these objectives is conveyed in the generator's input prompts. These findings suggest that DPG is a powerful and flexible technique for shaping model properties using only synthetic training examples.

</details>


### [102] [TEMPER: Testing Emotional Perturbation in Quantitative Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07801)
*Atahan Dokme,Benjamin Reichman,Larry Heck*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Emotional framing in queries reduces reasoning accuracy in large language models by 2-10 percentage points, despite no changes to numerical content. Neutralizing emotions mitigates this drop effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate the impact of emotional framing on the quantitative reasoning tasks of large language models commonly trained and evaluated on neutral language.

Method: Developed a controlled emotion translation framework to rewrite problems into emotional variants and constructed the Temper-5400 dataset to test eighteen models ranging from 1B to frontier scale.

Result: Emotional style reduces accuracy by 2-10%, while neutralizing emotional inputs recovers most lost performance, implicating emotional framing as the core issue.

Conclusion: Emotional framing degrades reasoning accuracy, but neutralization mitigates its effects, serving as a lightweight solution for real-world applications and suggesting a general framework for stylistic robustness tests.

Abstract: Large language models are trained and evaluated on quantitative reasoning tasks written in clean, emotionally neutral language. However, real-world queries are often wrapped in frustration, urgency or enthusiasm. Does emotional framing alone degrade reasoning when all numerical content is preserved? To investigate this, a controlled emotion translation framework is developed that rewrites problems into emotional variants while preserving all quantities and relationships. Using this framework, Temper-5400 (5,400 semantically verified emotion--neutral pairs) is constructed across GSM8K, MultiArith, and ARC-Challenge, and evaluated on eighteen models (1B to frontier scale). Two core results emerge: First, emotional framing reduces accuracy by 2-10 percentage points even though all numerical content is preserved. Second, neutralizing emotional variants recovers most of the lost performance, showing both that the degradation is tied to emotional style rather than content corruption and that neutralization can serve as a lightweight inference-time mitigation. Non-emotional paraphrases cause no such degradation, implicating emotional content rather than surface-level changes. Beyond emotion specifically, the benchmark construction procedure provides a general framework for controlled stylistic translation and robustness evaluation.

</details>


### [103] [Cram Less to Fit More: Training Data Pruning Improves Memorization of Facts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.08519)
*Jiayuan Ye,Vitaly Feldman,Kunal Talwar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper addresses large language models' struggles with memorizing facts and proposes a data selection method to improve fact accuracy, effectively optimizing models' capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models often face challenges in memorizing factual knowledge, which can lead to hallucinations and suboptimal performance, highlighting the need to understand and enhance fact accuracy.

Method: The study introduces an information-theoretic framework to investigate the effects of training data distribution on fact memorization. It proposes a data selection strategy that curates training data by limiting facts and flattening fact frequency distributions based on training loss.

Result: The proposed data selection method helps boost fact accuracy to the model's capacity limit on semi-synthetic datasets. Additionally, it enables smaller models to achieve performance equivalent to much larger ones during pretraining on an annotated Wikipedia corpus.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that optimizing data selection can significantly enhance factual memorization, allowing smaller models to perform competitively against larger models, with implications for efficient model training.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can struggle to memorize factual knowledge in their parameters, often leading to hallucinations and poor performance on knowledge-intensive tasks. In this paper, we formalize fact memorization from an information-theoretic perspective and study how training data distributions affect fact accuracy. We show that fact accuracy is suboptimal (below the capacity limit) whenever the amount of information contained in the training data facts exceeds model capacity. This is further exacerbated when the fact frequency distribution is skewed (e.g. a power law).
  We propose data selection schemes based on the training loss alone that aim to limit the number of facts in the training data and flatten their frequency distribution. On semi-synthetic datasets containing high-entropy facts, our selection method effectively boosts fact accuracy to the capacity limit. When pretraining language models from scratch on an annotated Wikipedia corpus, our selection method enables a GPT2-Small model (110m parameters) to memorize 1.3X more entity facts compared to standard training, matching the performance of a 10X larger model (1.3B parameters) pretrained on the full dataset.

</details>


### [104] [GRASS: Gradient-based Adaptive Layer-wise Importance Sampling for Memory-efficient Large Language Model Fine-tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07808)
*Kaiyuan Tian,Yu Tang,Gongqingjian Jiang,Baihui Liu,Yifu Gao,Xialin Su,Linbo Qiao,Dongsheng Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GRASS, a new framework, addresses GPU memory constraints in large language model fine-tuning by using adaptive layer-wise importance sampling and optimizer state offloading, outperforming existing methods in accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for fine-tuning large language models are constrained by GPU memory and often lead to suboptimal performance due to static layer importance sampling and limited expressiveness.

Method: The authors propose GRASS, which uses mean gradient norms for dynamic layer importance evaluation, adaptive training strategies for layer sampling probabilities, and a layer-wise optimizer state offloading mechanism to optimize memory efficiency.

Result: Extensive experimental findings indicate GRASS achieves up to 4.38% higher accuracy and reduces memory consumption by up to 19.97% across various models and benchmarks.

Conclusion: GRASS provides a significant improvement in both performance and efficiency over existing fine-tuning methods for large language models, demonstrating task-specific adaptivity and memory optimization principles.

Abstract: Full-parameter fine-tuning of large language models is constrained by substantial GPU memory requirements. Low-rank adaptation methods mitigate this challenge by updating only a subset of parameters. However, these approaches often limit model expressiveness and yield lower performance than full-parameter fine-tuning. Layer-wise fine-tuning methods have emerged as an alternative, enabling memory-efficient training through static layer importance sampling strategies. However, these methods overlook variations in layer importance across tasks and training stages, resulting in suboptimal performance on downstream tasks. To address these limitations, we propose GRASS, a gradient-based adaptive layer-wise importance sampling framework. GRASS utilizes mean gradient norms as a task-aware and training-stage-aware metric for estimating layer importance. Furthermore, GRASS adaptively adjusts layer sampling probabilities through an adaptive training strategy. We also introduce a layer-wise optimizer state offloading mechanism that overlaps computation and communication to further reduce memory usage while maintaining comparable training throughput. Extensive experiments across multiple models and benchmarks demonstrate that GRASS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving an average accuracy improvement of up to 4.38 points and reducing memory usage by up to 19.97\%.

</details>


### [105] [AsyncTLS: Efficient Generative LLM Inference with Asynchronous Two-level Sparse Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07815)
*Yuxuan Hu,Jianchao Tan,Jiaqi Zhang,Wen Zan,Pingwei Sun,Yifan Lu,Yerui Sun,Yuchen Xie,Xunliang Cai,Jing Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AsyncTLS addresses long-context inference issues in LLMs by combining hierarchical sparse attention and asynchronous KV cache offloading, offering both accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to resolve the dual challenges of high computational complexity and memory issues in long-context processing within LLMs.

Method: They introduce AsyncTLS, a hybrid approach that integrates block-level filtering, token-level sparse attention, and asynchronous KV cache handling to optimize performance.

Result: AsyncTLS delivers accuracy comparable to full attention models while achieving significant performance improvements in operator speedups (1.2x - 10.0x) and end-to-end throughput (1.3x - 4.7x).

Conclusion: AsyncTLS demonstrates a commendable balance between computational efficiency and accuracy, solving key bottlenecks in long-context LLM applications.

Abstract: Long-context inference in LLMs faces the dual challenges of quadratic attention complexity and prohibitive KV cache memory. While token-level sparse attention offers superior accuracy, its indexing overhead is costly; block-level methods improve efficiency but sacrifice precision. We propose AsyncTLS, a hierarchical sparse attention system that combines coarse-grained block filtering with fine-grained token selection to balance accuracy and efficiency, coupled with an asynchronous offloading engine that overlaps KV cache transfers with computation via temporal locality exploitation. Evaluated on Qwen3 and GLM-4.7-Flash across GQA, and MLA architectures, AsyncTLS achieves accuracy comparable to full attention while delivering 1.2x - 10.0x operator speedups and 1.3x - 4.7x end-to-end throughput improvements on 48k - 96k contexts.

</details>


### [106] [Tool Retrieval Bridge: Aligning Vague Instructions with Retriever Preferences via Bridge Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07816)
*Kunfeng Chen,Luyao Zhuang,Fei Liao,Juhua Liu,Jian Wang,Bo Du*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a tool retrieval method—Tool Retrieval Bridge (TRB)—to address the challenge of translating vague real-world tool instructions into actionable specifics, significantly improving performance in tool retrieval benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap between real-world vague tool instructions and the overly detailed instructions commonly used in academic tool benchmarks, which hinders effective tool retrieval for large language models.

Method: The authors construct a new benchmark, VGToolBench, to mimic vague human instructions and propose the Tool Retrieval Bridge (TRB) model. TRB rewrites vague instructions into specific ones to bridge the gap between ambiguous instructions and retrieval systems.

Result: TRB substantially improves tool retrieval performance; for example, it enhances BM25's relative performance by up to 111.51%, increasing the average NDCG score from 9.73 to 19.59 in experiments.

Conclusion: TRB effectively mitigates the impact of vague instructions in tool retrieval settings, improving performance across multiple retrievers, and provides an impactful solution for real-world applications. The source code and benchmarks are publicly released.

Abstract: Tool learning has emerged as a promising paradigm for large language models (LLMs) to address real-world challenges. Due to the extensive and irregularly updated number of tools, tool retrieval for selecting the desired tool subset is essential. However, current tool retrieval methods are usually based on academic benchmarks containing overly detailed instructions (e.g., specific API names and parameters), while real-world instructions are more vague. Such a discrepancy would hinder the tool retrieval in real-world applications. In this paper, we first construct a new benchmark, VGToolBench, to simulate human vague instructions. Based on this, we conduct a series of preliminary analyses and find that vague instructions indeed damage the performance of tool retrieval. To this end, we propose a simple-yet-effective Tool Retrieval Bridge (TRB) approach to boost the performance of tool retrieval for vague instructions. The principle of TRB is to introduce a bridge model to rewrite the vague instructions into more specific ones and alleviate the gap between vague instructions and retriever preferences.We conduct extensive experiments under multiple commonly used retrieval settings, and the results show that TRB effectively mitigates the ambiguity of vague instructions while delivering consistent and substantial improvements across all baseline retrievers. For example, with the help of TRB, BM25 achieves a relative improvement of up to 111.51%, i.e., increasing the average NDCG score from 9.73 to 19.59. The source code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/kfchenhn/TRB.

</details>


### [107] [Loop, Think, & Generalize: Implicit Reasoning in Recurrent-Depth Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07822)
*Harsh Kohli,Srinivasan Parthasarathy,Huan Sun,Yuekun Yao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Examines how recurrent-depth transformers improve implicit multi-hop reasoning compared to vanilla transformers, overcoming compositional generalization challenges like systematic generalization and depth extrapolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models struggle to combine parametric knowledge for implicit multi-hop reasoning, exposing limitations in compositional generalization.

Method: Recurrent-depth transformers enable iterative computation over transformer layers to address systematic generalization and depth extrapolation challenges.

Result: Recurrent-depth transformers outperform vanilla transformers, achieving compositional generalization through a structured grokking process and improved depth extrapolation by scaling inference-time recurrence.

Conclusion: Recurrent-depth transformers enhance multi-hop reasoning, but challenges like overthinking during excessive recurrence remain, requiring refined training strategies for improved effectiveness.

Abstract: We study implicit reasoning, i.e. the ability to combine knowledge or rules within a single forward pass. While transformer-based large language models store substantial factual knowledge and rules, they often fail to compose this knowledge for implicit multi-hop reasoning, suggesting a lack of compositional generalization over their parametric knowledge. To address this limitation, we study recurrent-depth transformers, which enables iterative computation over the same transformer layers. We investigate two compositional generalization challenges under the implicit reasoning scenario: systematic generalization, i.e. combining knowledge that is never used for compositions during training, and depth extrapolation, i.e. generalizing from limited reasoning depth (e.g. training on up to 5-hop) to deeper compositions (e.g. 10-hop). Through controlled studies with models trained from scratch, we show that while vanilla transformers struggle with both generalization challenges, recurrent-depth transformers can effectively make such generalization. For systematic generalization, we find that this ability emerges through a three-stage grokking process, transitioning from memorization to in-distribution generalization and finally to systematic generalization, supported by mechanistic analysis. For depth extrapolation, we show that generalization beyond training depth can be unlocked by scaling inference-time recurrence, with more iterations enabling deeper reasoning. We further study how training strategies affect extrapolation, providing guidance on training recurrent-depth transformers, and identify a key limitation, overthinking, where excessive recurrence degrades predictions and limits generalization to very deep compositions.

</details>


### [108] [Why Are We Lonely? Leveraging LLMs to Measure and Understand Loneliness in Caregivers and Non-caregivers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07834)
*Michelle Damin Kim,Ellie S. Paek,Yufen Lin,Emily Mroz,Jane Chung,Jinho D. Choi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper uses LLMs to analyze loneliness in caregivers and non-caregivers on Reddit, achieving solid accuracy and demonstrating differences in loneliness causes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand loneliness in social media, particularly differences between caregivers and non-caregivers, and establish a pipeline for creating related datasets.

Method: Developed expert loneliness evaluation frameworks and typologies, utilized GPT models in a validated pipeline to build a Reddit corpus and analyze data.

Result: Achieved accuracy rates of ~76%-79% for loneliness evaluation and ~0.80 F1 scores for cause categorization. Found notable differences in causes of loneliness for the two groups.

Conclusion: LLM-based pipelines can construct quality social media datasets for analyzing loneliness, revealing distinct loneliness causes in caregivers versus non-caregivers.

Abstract: This paper presents an LLM-driven approach for constructing diverse social media datasets to measure and compare loneliness in the caregiver and non-caregiver populations. We introduce an expert-developed loneliness evaluation framework and an expert-informed typology for categorizing causes of loneliness for analyzing social media text. Using a human-validated data processing pipeline, we apply GPT-4o, GPT-5-nano, and GPT-5 to build a high-quality Reddit corpus and analyze loneliness across both populations. The loneliness evaluation framework achieved average accuracies of 76.09% and 79.78% for caregivers and non-caregivers, respectively. The cause categorization framework achieved micro-aggregate F1 scores of 0.825 and 0.80 for caregivers and non-caregivers, respectively. Across populations, we observe substantial differences in the distribution of types of causes of loneliness. Caregivers' loneliness were predominantly linked to caregiving roles, identity recognition, and feelings of abandonment, indicating distinct loneliness experiences between the two groups. Demographic extraction further demonstrates the viability of Reddit for building a diverse caregiver loneliness dataset. Overall, this work establishes an LLM-based pipeline for creating high quality social media datasets for studying loneliness and demonstrates its effectiveness in analyzing population-level differences in the manifestation of loneliness.

</details>


### [109] [MemReader: From Passive to Active Extraction for Long-Term Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07877)
*Jingyi Kang,Chunyu Li,Ding Chen,Bo Tang,Feiyu Xiong,Zhiyu Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces MemReader, a family of advanced memory extractors designed to enhance structured long-term memory in agents, addressing challenges like noise, inconsistency, and referencing issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous agents require robust long-term memory for personalization and decision-making, yet existing methods for memory extraction handle noisy and incomplete data poorly, leading to inefficiencies and inconsistencies.

Method: Two models were developed: MemReader-0.6B, a distillation-based passive extractor ensuring schema-consistent outputs, and MemReader-4B, an active agent leveraging Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to actively select, defer, retrieve, or discard memory writes.

Result: Experiments reveal that MemReader significantly outperforms prior baselines in memory extraction, achieving state-of-the-art results in updating knowledge, handling temporal reasoning, and reducing hallucination.

Conclusion: Effective agent memory depends not on accumulating more data, but on selective and reasoning-driven extraction processes to build low-noise, adaptive long-term memory systems. The technology is deployed in real-world applications with publicly available models and APIs for further research.

Abstract: Long-term memory is fundamental for personalized and autonomous agents, yet populating it remains a bottleneck. Existing systems treat memory extraction as a one-shot, passive transcription from context to structured entries, which struggles with noisy dialogue, missing references, and cross-turn dependencies, leading to memory pollution, low-value writes, and inconsistency. In this paper, we introduce the MemReader family for active long-term memory extraction in agent systems: MemReader-0.6B, a compact and cost-efficient passive extractor distilled for accurate and schema-consistent structured outputs, and MemReader-4B, an active extractor optimized with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to make memory writing decisions. Under a ReAct-style paradigm, MemReader-4B explicitly evaluates information value, reference ambiguity, and completeness before acting, and can selectively write memories, defer incomplete inputs, retrieve historical context, or discard irrelevant chatter. Experiments on LOCOMO, LongMemEval, and HaluMem show that MemReader consistently outperforms existing extraction-based baselines. In particular, MemReader-4B achieves state-of-the-art performance on tasks involving knowledge updating, temporal reasoning, and hallucination reduction. These results suggest that effective agent memory requires not merely extracting more information, but performing reasoning-driven and selective memory extraction to build low-noise and dynamically evolving long-term memory. Furthermore, MemReader has been integrated into MemOS and is being deployed in real-world applications. To support future research and adoption, we release the models and provide public API access.

</details>


### [110] [Contextualising (Im)plausible Events Triggers Figurative Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07885)
*Annerose Eichel,Tonmoy Rakshit,Sabine Schulte im Walde*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates the link between (non-)literalness and plausibility in English events, comparing human and LLM evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how humans and language models perceive plausibility and non-literalness in language can enhance linguistic and AI research.

Method: The study uses a systematic setup involving plausible/implausible event triples combined with abstract/concrete categories, analyzing human and LLM judgments and contexts.

Result: Humans showcase advanced detection and interpretation, while LLMs exhibit shallow contextual understanding and a bias towards plausible interpretations.

Conclusion: Humans outperform LLMs in contextualizing and discerning (non-)literal events compared to implausible ones, highlighting significant assessment differences.

Abstract: This work explores the connection between (non-)literalness and plausibility at the example of subject-verb-object events in English. We design a systematic setup of plausible and implausible event triples in combination with abstract and concrete constituent categories. Our analysis of human and LLM-generated judgments and example contexts reveals substantial differences between assessments of plausibility. While humans excel at nuanced detection and contextualization of (non-)literal vs. implausible events, LLM results reveal only shallow contextualization patterns with a bias to trade implausibility for non-literal, plausible interpretations.

</details>


### [111] [Linear Representations of Hierarchical Concepts in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07886)
*Masaki Sakata,Benjamin Heinzerling,Takumi Ito,Sho Yokoi,Kentaro Inui*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates how hierarchical relations are encoded in language models and demonstrates a linear recoverability of these relations within a low-dimensional, domain-specific subspace.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study seeks to uncover the mechanisms by which language models encode hierarchical relationships, such as geographical or semantic hierarchies, and to understand the structure of their internal representations.

Method: The authors employ Linear Relational Concepts, training linear transformations specific to hierarchical depth and semantic domains, and examine how hierarchical information is distributed across different layers and multi-token representations.

Result: Findings reveal that hierarchical relations can be linearly extracted from language model representations, and these relations form a low-dimensional subspace that is largely domain-specific. Domain-specific subspaces, however, exhibit a high degree of similarity.

Conclusion: Hierarchical information is encoded in language models in interpretable, low-dimensional subspaces that vary by domain but remain fundamentally similar in structure, suggesting an efficient and consistent internal organization.

Abstract: We investigate how and to what extent hierarchical relations (e.g., Japan $\subset$ Eastern Asia $\subset$ Asia) are encoded in the internal representations of language models. Building on Linear Relational Concepts, we train linear transformations specific to each hierarchical depth and semantic domain, and characterize representational differences associated with hierarchical relations by comparing these transformations. Going beyond prior work on the representational geometry of hierarchies in LMs, our analysis covers multi-token entities and cross-layer representations. Across multiple domains we learn such transformations and evaluate in-domain generalization to unseen data and cross-domain transfer. Experiments show that, within a domain, hierarchical relations can be linearly recovered from model representations. We then analyze how hierarchical information is encoded in representation space. We find that it is encoded in a relatively low-dimensional subspace and that this subspace tends to be domain-specific. Our main result is that hierarchy representation is highly similar across these domain-specific subspaces. Overall, we find that all models considered in our experiments encode concept hierarchies in the form of highly interpretable linear representations.

</details>


### [112] [Data Selection for Multi-turn Dialogue Instruction Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07892)
*Bo Li,Shikun Zhang,Wei Ye*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces MDS, a framework for scoring and selecting high-quality multi-turn dialogue data. It addresses issues of noisy and inconsistent datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Instruction-tuned language models rely on multi-turn dialogue data, but existing datasets are often noisy with issues such as topic drift and mismatched formats, necessitating a better selection approach.

Method: The proposed MDS framework combines global and local evaluation stages: a global step to remove redundant dialogues and a local step to ensure topic grounding, information flow, and format consistency.

Result: MDS surpasses existing methods, including single-turn and dialogue-level scorers, in benchmarks and performs robustly on long conversations under similar resource constraints.

Conclusion: MDS improves selection of structured, high-quality dialogue datasets, boosting the viability and robustness of instruction-tuned language models.

Abstract: Instruction-tuned language models increasingly rely on large multi-turn dialogue corpora, but these datasets are often noisy and structurally inconsistent, with topic drift, repetitive chitchat, and mismatched answer formats across turns. We address this from a data selection perspective and propose \textbf{MDS} (Multi-turn Dialogue Selection), a dialogue-level framework that scores whole conversations rather than isolated turns. MDS combines a global coverage stage that performs bin-wise selection in the user-query trajectory space to retain representative yet non-redundant dialogues, with a local structural stage that evaluates within-dialogue reliability through entity-grounded topic grounding and information progress, together with query-answer form consistency for functional alignment. MDS outperforms strong single-turn selectors, dialogue-level LLM scorers, and heuristic baselines on three multi-turn benchmarks and an in-domain Banking test set, achieving the best overall rank across reference-free and reference-based metrics, and is more robust on long conversations under the same training budget. Code and resources are included in the supplementary materials.

</details>


### [113] [TSUBASA: Improving Long-Horizon Personalization via Evolving Memory and Self-Learning with Context Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07894)
*Xinliang Frederick Zhang,Lu Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces TSUBASA, enhancing personalized large language models' (PLLMs) long-horizon capabilities with dynamic memory evolution and self-learning techniques, demonstrating superior performance on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PLLMs face challenges in handling long-term tasks due to limitations in memory mechanisms, retrieval quality-efficiency tradeoffs, and data scarcity for parametric updates.

Method: TSUBASA improves PLLMs by employing dynamic memory evolution for better memory updates and self-learning using context distillation to internalize user experiences.

Result: TSUBASA demonstrated significant performance improvements on long-horizon tasks with reduced token budgets, outperforming state-of-the-art memory-augmented systems like Mem0 and Memory-R1.

Conclusion: The approach effectively overcomes current limitations of PLLMs in long-term tasks, achieving a balance between quality and efficiency to deliver enhanced personalization.

Abstract: Personalized large language models (PLLMs) have garnered significant attention for their ability to align outputs with individual's needs and preferences. However, they still struggle with long-horizon tasks, such as tracking a user's extensive history of conversations or activities. Existing memory mechanisms often fail to capture evolving behaviors, and RAG paradigms are trapped by a quality-efficiency tradeoff. Meanwhile, parametric adaptation is bottlenecked by train-inference gap due to the scarcity of labeled data. To enhance the long-horizon capabilities of PLLMs, we introduce TSUBASA, a two-pronged approach designed to improve memory writing via dynamic memory evolution, and memory reading via self-learning with a context distillation objective to internalize user experiences. Extensive evaluations on long-horizon benchmarks using the Qwen-3 model family (4B to 32B) validate the effectiveness of TSUBASA, surpassing competitive memory-augmented systems that rely primarily on memory writing, such as Mem0 and Memory-R1. Our analyses further confirms that TSUBASA breaks the quality-efficiency barrier to achieve Pareto improvements, delivering robust, high-fidelity personalization with a reduced token budget.

</details>


### [114] [HCRE: LLM-based Hierarchical Classification for Cross-Document Relation Extraction with a Prediction-then-Verification Strategy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07937)
*Guoqi Ma,Liang Zhang,Hongyao Tu,Hao Fu,Hui Li,Yujie Lin,Longyue Wang,Weihua Luo,Jinsong Su*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper explores Large Language Models (LLMs) for cross-document relation extraction (RE) and introduces a hierarchical classification model (HCRE) for improved performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current systems for cross-document RE using Small Language Models (SLMs) face limitations due to restricted language understanding abilities, motivating exploration of LLMs.

Method: The study proposes HCRE, combining LLMs for relation prediction with a hierarchical relation tree for classification, and introduces a prediction-then-verification strategy to mitigate error propagation.

Result: The proposed HCRE model improves cross-document RE performance and outperforms baseline approaches.

Conclusion: Hierarchical classification with LLMs provides a scalable solution for predefined relations in cross-document RE, enhancing reliability and effectiveness.

Abstract: Cross-document relation extraction (RE) aims to identify relations between the head and tail entities located in different documents. Existing approaches typically adopt the paradigm of ``\textit{Small Language Model (SLM) + Classifier}''. However, the limited language understanding ability of SLMs hinders further improvement of their performance. In this paper, we conduct a preliminary study to explore the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) in cross-document RE. Despite their extensive parameters, our findings indicate that LLMs do not consistently surpass existing SLMs. Further analysis suggests that the underperformance is largely attributed to the challenges posed by the numerous predefined relations. To overcome this issue, we propose an LLM-based \underline{H}ierarchical \underline{C}lassification model for cross-document \underline{RE} (HCRE), which consists of two core components: 1) an LLM for relation prediction and 2) a \textit{hierarchical relation tree} derived from the predefined relation set. This tree enables the LLM to perform hierarchical classification, where the target relation is inferred level by level. Since the number of child nodes is much smaller than the size of the entire predefined relation set, the hierarchical relation tree significantly reduces the number of relation options that LLM needs to consider during inference. However, hierarchical classification introduces the risk of error propagation across levels. To mitigate this, we propose a \textit{prediction-then-verification} inference strategy that improves prediction reliability through multi-view verification at each level. Extensive experiments show that HCRE outperforms existing baselines, validating its effectiveness.

</details>


### [115] [Large Language Model Post-Training: A Unified View of Off-Policy and On-Policy Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07941)
*Shiwan Zhao,Zhihu Wang,Xuyang Zhao,Jiaming Zhou,Caiyue Xu,Chenfei Liu,Liting Zhang,Yuhang Jia,Yanzhe Zhang,Hualong Yu,Zichen Xu,Qicheng Li,Yong Qin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper reviews methods for post-training large language models (LLMs), proposing a unified framework to address behavioral bottlenecks by organizing methods into support expansion, policy reshaping, and behavioral consolidation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address fragmentation in LLM post-training discussions by providing a unified framework that organizes methods based on how they intervene in model behavior, helping to diagnose bottlenecks and guide future developments.

Method: The authors introduce a framework that categorizes post-training methods into two learning regimes (off-policy and on-policy) and three roles: support expansion, policy reshaping, and behavioral consolidation. They analyze existing methods (e.g., SFT, RL, distillation) through this lens.

Result: The framework offers a new way to unify and interpret post-training paradigms. It provides insights into how different methods and hybrid pipelines contribute to behavior improvement and system-level design.

Conclusion: Progress in LLM post-training increasingly relies on coordinated multi-stage designs rather than single objectives. This framework aids in diagnosing bottlenecks and designing effective training pipelines.

Abstract: Post-training has become central to turning pretrained large language models (LLMs) into aligned and deployable systems. Recent progress spans supervised fine-tuning (SFT), preference optimization, reinforcement learning (RL), process supervision, verifier-guided methods, distillation, and multi-stage pipelines. Yet these methods are often discussed in fragmented ways, organized by labels or objective families rather than by the behavioral bottlenecks they address.
  This survey argues that LLM post-training is best understood as structured intervention on model behavior. We organize the field first by trajectory provenance, which defines two primary learning regimes: off-policy learning on externally supplied trajectories, and on-policy learning on learner-generated rollouts. We then interpret methods through two recurring roles -- effective support expansion, which makes useful behaviors more reachable, and policy reshaping, which improves behavior within already reachable regions -- together with a complementary systems-level role, behavioral consolidation, which preserves, transfers, and amortizes behavior across stages and model transitions.
  This perspective yields a unified reading of major paradigms. SFT may serve either support expansion or policy reshaping, whereas preference-based methods are usually off-policy reshaping. On-policy RL often improves behavior on learner-generated states, though under stronger guidance it can also make hard-to-reach reasoning paths reachable. Distillation is often best understood as consolidation rather than only compression, and hybrid pipelines emerge as coordinated multi-stage compositions.
  Overall, the framework helps diagnose post-training bottlenecks and reason about stage composition, suggesting that progress in LLM post-training increasingly depends on coordinated system design rather than any single dominant objective.

</details>


### [116] [Rethinking Data Mixing from the Perspective of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07963)
*Yuanjian Xu,Tianze Sun,Changwei Xu,XinLong Zhao,Jianing Hao,Ran Chen,Yang Liu,Ruijie Xu,Stephen Chen,Guang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper highlights the importance of data mixing strategies for LLM training, introduces theoretical insights, and proposes DoGraph as a new reweighting framework for data scheduling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There are gaps in understanding how data domains influence generalization in LLM training and misalignment exists between human and model perceptions of domains.

Method: The authors establish formal theoretical connections between gradient dynamics and domain distributions, then develop DoGraph, a graph-constrained optimization framework for data scheduling and reweighting.

Result: Experiments on GPT-2 models across different scales show that DoGraph consistently delivers competitive generalization performance compared to other methods.

Conclusion: DoGraph provides an effective approach to understand and optimize domain weighting in LLM training, addressing fundamental questions about domain perception and generalization.

Abstract: Data mixing strategy is essential for large language model (LLM) training. Empirical evidence shows that inappropriate strategies can significantly reduce generalization. Although recent methods have improved empirical performance, several fundamental questions remain open: what constitutes a domain, whether human and model perceptions of domains are aligned, and how domain weighting influences generalization. We address these questions by establishing formal connections between gradient dynamics and domain distributions, offering a theoretical framework that clarifies the role of domains in training dynamics. Building on this analysis, we introduce DoGraph, a reweighting framework that formulates data scheduling as a graph-constrained optimization problem. Extensive experiments on GPT-2 models of varying scales demonstrate that DoGraph consistently achieves competitive performance.

</details>


### [117] [AtomEval: Atomic Evaluation of Adversarial Claims in Fact Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07967)
*Hongyi Cen,Mingxin Wang,Yule Liu,Jingyi Zheng,Hanze Jia,Tan Tang,Yingcai Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces AtomEval to better evaluate adversarial claim rewriting in fact-checking by focusing on truth-conditional validity rather than surface similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing fact-checking evaluation metrics often fail to detect factual corruption in adversarial rewrites and label semantically invalid claims as correct.

Method: The authors propose AtomEval, a framework that decomposes claims into subject-relation-object-modifier (SROM) elements and scores them using Atomic Validity Scoring (AVS).

Result: Experiments using the FEVER dataset show AtomEval reliably identifies factual corruption across attack strategies and LLM-based adversarial claim generators.

Conclusion: AtomEval reveals limitations in how adversarial claims are evaluated, demonstrating that stronger LLMs don't always produce more valid adversarial claims.

Abstract: Adversarial claim rewriting is widely used to test fact-checking systems, but standard metrics fail to capture truth-conditional consistency and often label semantically corrupted rewrites as successful. We introduce AtomEval, a validity-aware evaluation framework that decomposes claims into subject-relation-object-modifier (SROM) atoms and scores adversarial rewrites with Atomic Validity Scoring (AVS), enabling detection of factual corruption beyond surface similarity. Experiments on the FEVER dataset across representative attack strategies and LLM generators show that AtomEval provides more reliable evaluation signals in our experiments. Using AtomEval, we further analyze LLM-based adversarial generators and observe that stronger models do not necessarily produce more effective adversarial claims under validity-aware evaluation, highlighting previously overlooked limitations in current adversarial evaluation practices.

</details>


### [118] [Kathleen: Oscillator-Based Byte-Level Text Classification Without Tokenization or Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07969)
*George Fountzoulas*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Kathleen, a lightweight text classification model based on frequency-domain processing that operates on raw UTF-8 bytes without tokenization or an attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with just 733K parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an efficient text classification model with minimal resource usage, avoiding tokenization and attention mechanisms, while leveraging frequency-domain processing for improved accuracy and computational efficiency.

Method: Kathleen introduces three key innovations: (1) RecurrentOscillatorBanks for O(L) sequence processing with temporal memory; (2) FFT-Rotate Wavetable Encoder for compact input encoding; and (3) PhaseHarmonics, a sinusoidal non-linearity with significant impact on accuracy.

Result: The Kathleen-Clean model achieves impressive results: 88.6% on IMDB, 92.3% on AG News, and 83.3% on SST-2. It outperforms a tokenized model with 16x more parameters, while maintaining O(L) time and memory efficiency.

Conclusion: Kathleen demonstrates that frequency-domain processing can outperform traditional attention-based models in text classification, achieving better accuracy with fewer parameters and lower computational overhead.

Abstract: We present Kathleen, a text classification architecture that operates directly on raw UTF-8 bytes using frequency-domain processing -- requiring no tokenizer, no attention mechanism, and only 733K parameters. Kathleen introduces three novel components: (1) RecurrentOscillatorBanks -- damped sinusoid convolutions with temporal memory for O(L) sequence processing; (2) an FFT-Rotate Wavetable Encoder that maps all 256 byte values using a single learnable vector (256 floats), replacing conventional embedding tables (65K parameters) while improving accuracy; (3) PhaseHarmonics -- a sinusoidal non-linearity with just 6 learnable phase parameters that our ablation identifies as the single most impactful component (+2.6% accuracy, <0.001% of model parameters). Through comprehensive ablation of a 1.8M-parameter predecessor, we show that frequency-domain components systematically outperform complex cognitive architectures: removing a 560K-parameter bio-inspired framework costs only -0.2%, while removing the 6-parameter PhaseHarmonics costs -2.6%.
